---
title: "Command Contracts and Semantic Interfaces"
chapter_id: "command-contracts-and-semantic-interfaces"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "software_magic_grimoire"
  - "viea"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "cognitive_compilation"
  - "talos"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "All five assigned local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; support remains argument until parser, dispatch, prompt-injection, or execution tests exist."
  - "Only protocol schema/fixture validation is implemented; no behavioral execution tests have been run for this chapter."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `command-contracts-and-semantic-interfaces` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `software_magic_grimoire`, `viea`, `genesiscode`, `cognitive_compilation`, `talos` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `software_magic_grimoire`, `viea`; supporting: `genesiscode`, `cognitive_compilation`, `talos` |
| Source loading state | source notes and passage-reviewed local raw cache: `software_magic_grimoire`, `viea`, `genesiscode`, `cognitive_compilation`, `talos` |
| Test state | `command_contract.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.CommandContracts` implements finite required-field and explicit-constraint precedence gates; behavioral execution, prompt-injection, and dispatch-blocking tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats prompt language as an operational interface only when it is bounded by explicit fields. The repository validates a command-contract fixture, but that validation proves shape only; it does not prove that a prompt is secure, complete, injection-proof, or empirically better than an unstructured prompt.

It follows the execution-trace chapter by zooming into the first machine-readable handoff: the command surface that lets planning proceed without guessing what the user meant or what authority was granted.

This chapter is where the stack stops treating language as a vibe and starts treating it as an interface. The prior intent contract says what was authorized; the command contract says how that authorization is shaped into fields that planners, validators, and job runners can actually inspect.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

An intent-to-execution trace still needs a first machine-readable handoff: the command contract. It is the point where ordinary language becomes structured enough that planning can proceed without guessing at hidden requirements or hidden authority.

The important move is not to make human language robotic. It is to preserve what matters: role, objective, context, constraints, output, verification, failure behavior, and permission boundary. A good command contract lets the stack be helpful while still knowing when it must ask, refuse, or stop. It also gives later layers something concrete to inspect when a plan drifts from the original purpose. This is the earliest place to catch mismatched expectations.

The contract protects both sides: the person keeps intent legible, and the machine receives bounded work instead of open-ended permission. A clear command is less glamorous than a clever prompt, but it is much easier to govern.

The interface is where helpfulness becomes a bounded obligation. A command that cannot be refused has not been typed tightly enough. Refusal completes the type because unsafe commands need a shaped exit.
:::

## Problem

The stack needs compact command language that is expressive enough for AI cooperation and precise enough for validation.

Intent has become a trace. The control language inside that trace is the command contract. Software Magic Grimoire supplies the vocabulary: technical words compress mechanisms, responsibilities, workflows, failure shadows, and next actions. VIEA supplies the contract shape: role, objective, context, constraints, procedure, output, verification, and failure behavior.

A command contract is not a prettier prompt. It is the smallest artifact that lets a planner, verifier, tool runner, reviewer, or later agent understand what kind of work has been authorized and what would count as failure.

The key property is contract precedence. Helpful context, style notes, examples, retrieved passages, and prior conversation can inform the work, but they should not silently rewrite objective, constraints, authority, verification, or failure behavior. The command contract is the semantic firewall between useful context and operational permission.

This firewall has to be visible at field level. A command can look structured while still smuggling ambiguity into the fields. The stack should therefore record where each field came from: user-confirmed, system-inferred, source-derived, defaulted, policy-imposed, or missing. Field provenance is what lets planning distinguish a real grant from a plausible fill-in.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Prompt prose often hides objectives, constraints, output contracts, and failure behavior in unstructured language.

Free-form prompts are useful for exploration, but they are weak as operational boundaries. They mix discovery, design, implementation, verification, and recovery into one text blob. They also invite hidden instruction conflicts: a context fragment can smuggle a command that overrides the actual contract unless explicit fields dominate implicit prose.

GenesisCode adds a useful analogy from software: privileged effects and control responses need recognizable protocol shapes. In the ASI Stack, a command must expose output and verification requirements before it can be lowered into planning or execution.

Unstructured prompts also make error recovery harder. If a result fails, the system cannot tell whether the failure came from a bad objective, missing context, invalid procedure, unsupported tool, weak verification, or ambiguous output contract. A semantic interface makes failures addressable instead of merely disappointing.

Structured prompts can fail in the same way if they only move prose into boxes. A vague output field, inferred authority field, or missing failure behavior is not cured by being placed in YAML or JSON. The interface should require enough specificity that another layer can decide whether to dispatch, clarify, block, or create a residual.

## Core Claim

[command-contracts-and-semantic-interfaces.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Command contracts should use explicit semantic interfaces for objective, context, constraints, procedure, output, verification, and failure behavior.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Software Magic Grimoire supplies the semantic-density and bounded-command vocabulary; VIEA supplies the role/objective/context/constraint/output/verification envelope; GenesisCode supplies privileged effect and protocol-shape discipline; Cognitive Compilation and Talos show why the contract must lower cleanly into IR, typed jobs, and evidence records.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this core command-contract claim to all assigned source notes, and all five assigned local raw-cache mappings have been passage-reviewed. The reviewed passages support the explicit semantic-interface vocabulary, not prompt safety, parser completeness, dispatch correctness, or empirical productivity gains.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Passage-reviewed: instructions as bounded spells with roles, objectives, context, constraints, procedure, output, verification, failure behavior, guards, handoffs, loops, recursion, and recovery paths. | Promptcraft and vocabulary framework only; no prompt-safety or productivity result is proven. |
| `viea` | Passage-reviewed: structured command layer with role, objective, context, constraints, procedure, output contract, verification, failure behavior, artifacts, ledgers, routing, runtime adapters, and feedback. | Does not prove an automated semantic extractor, command dispatcher, injection defense, or execution correctness. |
| `genesiscode` | Passage-reviewed: privileged effects and control responses require recognizable protocol shapes: capability boundaries, replay logs, obligations, semantic patches, provenance hashes, and protocol seals. | No local implementation, replay checker, proof module, benchmark, or security audit validates the protocol discipline. |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Passage-reviewed: goals and constraints lower into source plans, semantic atoms, inputs, outputs, dependencies, validation requirements, compiler passes, and target artifacts. | No local compiler, parser, trace suite, or empirical ablation validates the lowering path. |
| `talos` | Passage-reviewed: contracts lower into typed jobs with contract locks, adjudication, delivery, feedback, evidence records, audit, replay, approvals, and controlled adapters. | Design source only; tool-effect enforcement, approval behavior, replay behavior, and benchmark/security claims are unverified here. |

## Mechanism

Software Magic Grimoire treats operative language as a bounded instruction with role, objective, context, constraints, procedure, output, verification, and failure behavior. VIEA turns that envelope into part of the intent-to-execution spine. GenesisCode explains why privileged effects need recognizable protocol shapes, while Cognitive Compilation and Talos explain why the same fields must lower cleanly into semantic IR, typed jobs, evidence, approval, and replay.

A Command Contract is the compact semantic interface that carries those fields forward without letting implicit prose become hidden authority.

The passage-reviewed sources support the field vocabulary and lowering pressure, not a completed command system. They justify treating objective, context, constraints, output, verification, failure behavior, protocol shape, semantic IR, and typed jobs as first-class interfaces. They do not show that free prose can be parsed correctly, that hostile context is blocked, that a dispatcher enforces constraints, or that structured prompts outperform unstructured prompts in a measured setting.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Natural-language request"] --> B["Semantic extraction"]
  B --> C["Role + objective"]
  C --> D["Context + constraints"]
  D --> E["Procedure + allowed means"]
  E --> F["Output contract"]
  F --> G["Verification contract"]
  G --> H{"Failure behavior declared?"}
  H -- "yes" --> I["Planning input"]
  H -- "no" --> J["Missing-contract residual"]
  J --> K["Clarification or contract repair"]
  K --> B
```

**Diagram reading note:** The flow shows a request being narrowed into contract fields before planning receives it. The loop from missing failure behavior back to extraction is the key: incomplete contracts should be repaired or clarified instead of silently becoming hidden authority.

The mechanism turns recurring command patterns into reusable templates and treats software words as coordination primitives. Field names such as objective, constraint, output, verification, authority, approval, and failure behavior are not decoration; they are routing handles for the rest of the stack. Binding those handles to schemas and tests is what lets later layers distinguish an authorized instruction from useful prose nearby.

The command contract should be compact enough to write often and typed enough to validate. Its field names matter because they become routing handles. "Verification" routes to evidence and tests. "Failure behavior" routes to residuals and escalation. "Allowed means" routes to authority. "Output contract" routes to artifact checks.

Promptcraft remains useful, but only inside this envelope. The command can include a procedure or style, yet those instructions cannot override explicit constraints, authority ceilings, source boundaries, or required verification.

The contract should also mark field confidence. Some fields are exact, some are inferred, some are defaulted, and some are missing. A planner should be able to distinguish "the user authorized this means" from "the system inferred a likely means" before it creates work. Missing or inferred authority should route to clarification, draft-only output, or residual rather than dispatch.

Command contracts should move through explicit validation states:

| State | Meaning | Allowed movement |
|---|---|---|
| `draft` | Fields are being extracted or normalized. | No planning dispatch. |
| `field_complete` | Required fields exist but may include inferred/defaulted values. | Validation and review only. |
| `conflict_detected` | Context, examples, hidden instructions, or fields conflict with explicit constraints. | Residual or clarification. |
| `authority_inferred` | A means, tool, disclosure, publication, or effect is inferred rather than granted. | Draft-only or re-contract. |
| `dispatch_blocked` | Required output, verification, failure, approval, or authority field is missing or vague. | No plan node dispatch. |
| `validated_for_planning` | Required fields are concrete and precedence review passed. | May lower into a plan graph. |
| `superseded` | A newer command contract replaces the current one. | Historical trace only. |

These states are intentionally pre-execution states. They do not say the work is correct. They only say whether the contract is clear enough to become planning input.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Command Contract.

- Planning consumes structured commands.
- Execution enforces output contracts.
- Verification checks stated failure behavior.

Minimum fields:

- `contract_id`
- `intent_id`
- `validation_state`
- `role`
- `objective`
- `context_refs`
- `field_provenance`
- `field_confidence`
- `constraints`
- `procedure`
- `allowed_means`
- `forbidden_means`
- `bounded_defaults`
- `output_contract`
- `verification`
- `failure_behavior`
- `authority_ceiling`
- `authority_basis`
- `required_approvals`
- `expected_artifacts`
- `feedback_route`
- `recontract_points`
- `dispatch_blockers`
- `non_claims`

Planning consumes the structured command. Execution enforces allowed means and output contracts. Verification checks the declared criteria and failure behavior. Evidence records which fields were satisfied, repaired, escalated, or left residual.

This interface is deliberately narrower than natural language. It does not attempt to preserve every nuance of the request; it preserves the operational fields that downstream layers need to avoid guessing. The raw request can remain attached for review, but the structured fields control dispatch.

The command contract schema captures validation state, field provenance, field confidence, bounded defaults, authority basis, re-contract points, dispatch blockers, and non-claims. That is still only a protocol surface. It does not prove that a semantic extractor found the fields correctly, that hostile context was blocked, or that a dispatcher enforced the result. It gives those future tests stable hooks.

Each field should carry confidence/provenance metadata:

| Field status | Meaning | Dispatch rule |
|---|---|---|
| `confirmed` | Explicitly stated or accepted by the user/governance boundary. | May support planning. |
| `policy_imposed` | Added by active policy, constitution, or safety rule. | May constrain planning; cannot broaden authority. |
| `source_derived` | Taken from an authorized source artifact. | May support context or requirements within source limits. |
| `defaulted` | Filled by a bounded default. | Must carry scope and re-contract trigger. |
| `inferred` | Guessed from context or pattern. | Cannot authorize side effects. |
| `missing` | Required value absent. | Blocks dispatch or narrows output to clarification/draft. |

This metadata keeps field confidence from becoming hidden evidence. A field can be useful and still too weak to authorize work.

## Invariants

- A command exposes output and verification requirements.
- Failure behavior is declared.
- Implicit instructions do not override explicit constraints.
- Field provenance and confidence remain visible to planning and verification.
- Inferred or defaulted authority cannot authorize side effects.

The override rule is central: context can inform a command, but it cannot silently replace explicit constraints. If a source, tool, user message, or retrieved note conflicts with the command contract, the system should emit a conflict residual instead of choosing the most fluent interpretation.

A related invariant is output-test coupling. A command with no artifact expectation or verification rule is not ready for governed planning, because the downstream system cannot know what success or failure should look like.

## Failure modes

- Semantic ambiguity.
- Prompt injection through context.
- Unspecified output contract.
- Field laundering, where vague prose is moved into a formal field without becoming testable.
- Authority inference, where a likely means is treated as if the human granted it.

Semantic ambiguity produces false agreement: everyone thinks the command is clear until the artifact arrives. Prompt injection through context attacks the contract boundary. An unspecified output contract makes verification subjective. All three should become missing-contract or conflict records before planning proceeds.

The subtle failure is field laundering: a vague sentence is copied into a formal field, making it look structured without becoming testable. A contract field should be concrete enough that a later verifier can say whether it was satisfied, violated, or left residual.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A canonical command-contract template used by future chapter drafting and experiment prompts.

The semantic interface begins as a public-safe `command_contract` record. That record demonstrates validation state, field provenance, field confidence, constraints, bounded defaults, output, verification, approvals, authority basis, re-contract points, dispatch blockers, non-claims, and failure behavior while leaving command execution unclaimed.

That fixture is not a hardened prompt firewall. It is the first public contract shape that later prompt-injection tests, override tests, and execution traces can target.

A small command-conflict fixture set is the right first stress test: one clean command, one missing output contract, one hidden-context override, one inferred-authority case, and one declared failure-behavior case. The set would let the book demonstrate contract precedence while keeping full prompt-security behavior out of scope.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Command contracts become mature when they serve as the semantic control language for governed work. They are not a prompt-template library; they are the small, inspectable language that humans, planners, verifiers, job runners, policy layers, and future AI agents use to agree on what the next unit of work means before any execution path opens.

Inside the semantic interface, every command field has provenance, confidence, precedence, and dispatch consequence. A command can carry natural language, examples, style, and source context, but those materials cannot overwrite explicit objective, constraints, authority, output, verification, or failure behavior. Context informs the contract; it does not become a hidden command channel.

A semantic command surface needs:

- A typed command vocabulary for objective, scope, context references, constraints, allowed means, forbidden means, output contract, verifier, approval path, failure behavior, feedback route, and non-claims.
- Field-level provenance that distinguishes confirmed, policy-imposed, source-derived, defaulted, inferred, and missing values.
- A precedence checker that detects context overrides, vague formal fields, inferred authority, incompatible examples, and output contracts that cannot be verified.
- Lowering rules that let validated commands become plan inputs, while draft, conflicting, missing, or inferred-authority commands remain clarification requests, review items, or residuals.
- Reusable command templates for recurring work, each versioned with its expected artifacts, guards, recovery path, and evidence burden.

This semantic firewall remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. Keep the support boundary at `argument` until parser behavior, conflict checks, override tests, dispatch enforcement, and downstream traces show that rich human instruction can be understood without letting unvalidated meaning cross into execution.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Command contract fixture validation | Check that the command-contract fixture contains objective, constraints, output, verification, authority, approvals, artifacts, and failure behavior required by the schema. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Command schema validation test | Check that every command exposes objective, constraints, output contract, verification, and failure behavior. | planned; not run |
| Failure-behavior declaration test | Check that missing or vague failure behavior blocks planning dispatch. | planned; not run |
| Prompt override scenario | Check that hidden or conflicting context instructions cannot override explicit constraints. | planned; not run |
| Field-confidence audit | Check that confirmed, policy-imposed, source-derived, defaulted, inferred, and missing fields have different dispatch consequences. | planned; not run |
| Authority-inference block test | Check that inferred means, tools, disclosures, publications, or effects cannot lower into executable plans without re-contracting. | planned; not run |

Only the fixture-shape check is implemented. The override and dispatch-blocking tests need a parser, conflict rule, or harness that can compare explicit fields against retrieved context and hidden instructions.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:command.semantic_interface.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.CommandContracts` | A valid command contract contains objective, constraints, output contract, verification, and failure behavior. | implemented |
| `lean:command.semantic_interface.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.CommandContracts` | A hidden or conflicting instruction cannot override an explicit contract constraint. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over command field and constraint-override reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: the required interface fields are present, and a hidden or conflicting instruction cannot override an explicit constraint when the precedence review applies. They do not prove prompt-injection resistance, parser completeness, semantic extraction quality, or behavioral dispatch blocking.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Software Magic Grimoire | command_contracts_promptcraft | Structured command contracts and semantic density of software words. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Cognitive Compilation | planning_semantic_ir | Compiler framing for LLM-centered planning, semantic IR, target compilation, incremental repair. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |

The command-envelope sources support the chapter's lowering pressure: ordinary instruction must become bounded fields before planning or dispatch can rely on it. All assigned mappings have passage review, but the core claim stays at `argument` until parser behavior, dispatch behavior, override handling, or empirical productivity/safety evidence is recorded through accepted evidence transitions.

## Summary

Command contracts and semantic interfaces give the stack an operational language. They compress intent into fields that planners, tools, validators, and reviewers can all read without inheriting hidden authority.

The discipline is explicitness under compression: make commands short enough to use, but structured enough that failure, verification, and authority remain visible. With the command surface explicit, accepted work can become a governed plan before any side effect occurs.

The handoff is precise: planning may decompose the command, but it may not turn context into authority, style into requirement, or helpful inference into permission. That is how the stack keeps language useful without letting it become ambient control. A command should also be boring enough for machines to reject. Missing fields, invalid grants, unsupported assumptions, expired context, and unsafe effects should be ordinary validation failures. That is the practical value of the interface: it reduces the amount of judgment required before the system can say no, ask for clarification, escalate, or continue under a narrower contract.

## Handoff

Once a command exposes objective, constraints, output contract, verification, and failure behavior, the stack can ask how accepted work becomes a controlled sequence rather than a single leap into action. Planning as a Control Layer takes the validated command surface and turns it into dependencies, budgets, context requests, authority limits, blocked-node records, and stop conditions before any executable job exists.
